svg.text: CPU Scheduling Basic Concepts Scheduling Criteria

section.text: Introduction
CPU scheduling is the process of allocating the CPU to a process or thread and determining the order in which they are executed.
Basic Concepts
CPU Scheduling Models
Preemptive Scheduling: The operating system can interrupt a process and switch to another process at any time.
Non-Preemptive Scheduling: Once a process is scheduled, it runs until it is completed.
CPU Scheduling Types
Short-Term Scheduling: Allocation of the CPU to a process for a short period of time.
Long-Term Scheduling: Allocation of resources to a process for an extended period of time.
Multilevel Scheduling: Allocation of resources to a process based on multiple criteria.
Scheduling Criteria
Factors Affecting CPU Scheduling
Process Priority: The priority of a process determines the order in which it is executed.
Process Arrival Time: The time at which a process arrives at the operating system determines its priority.
Process Burst Time: The time a process runs on the CPU determines its priority.
CPU Utilization: The amount of time the CPU is used determines the priority of a process.
Scheduling Algorithms
First-Come-First-Served
FCFS: The process with the earliest arrival time is executed first.
Shortest Job First
SJF: The process with the shortest burst time is executed first.
Priority Scheduling
PS: The process with the highest priority is executed first.
Round Robin
RR: Each process is given a fixed time slice to execute.
